query: prevalence of complex coronary lesions in diabetics

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Each complex coronary artery lesion has a unique set of etiologies. For example, bifurcation lesions are primarily anatomy-based. [12] Coronary artery calcifications can result from inherent inflammatory processes, as well as the activity of osteoblastic activity. [13] The presence of a thrombus can often result from plaque disruption before or during PCI. [14] Saphenous vein graft occlusion is thought to be a process of intimal vessel smooth muscle proliferation. [15] There are disease processes that are common to each disorder. For example, Baris et al. identified diabetes mellitus and advanced age as independent predictors for all complex coronary lesions. [16] Other risk factors for coronary artery disease and atherosclerosis, such as age, hypertension, obesity, male sex, smoking, and hypercholesterolemia, can be inferred but have not directly been identified in any significant studies as independent risk factors for complex lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Restenosis of Stented Coronary Arteries -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The ISR rate is often higher in the setting of multivessel disease compared with single-vessel disease, as demonstrated in the study of Zhao et al; the occurrence of ISR was significantly higher in patients with two-vessel (OR: 2.922; 95% CI: 1.266–6.745; P=0.012) or three-vessel disease (OR: 2.574; 95% CI: 1.128–5.872; P=0.025) when compared with those with one-vessel disease. [10] [3] In a separate study with angiographic follow-up of 10004 patients who had stenting, implantation of first-generation DES versus BMS (OR 0.35, 95% CI 0.31 to 0.39), and second-generation DES versus first-generation DES (OR 0.67, 95% CI 0.58 to 0.77) were found to be independent predictors of lower rates of restenosis. [11] Multivariate analysis identified small vessel size, total stent length, complex lesion morphology, presence of diabetes mellitus, and history of bypass surgery as independent predictors of worse restenosis outcomes, and these were similar across the spectrum of stent devices. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Complex coronary lesions carry higher overall mortality in patients with stable angina than those with noncomplex lesions. These complex lesions are often associated with diabetes, lower ventricular ejection fraction, and lower HDL-C. The SYNTAX Score and SYNTAX Score II are frequently the means to decide on the best revascularization approach. These scoring systems are widely used and have been validated in many studies. [52] [53] [54] Studies have shown SYNTAX Score II to be superior to the SYNTAX score as a prognostic tool.

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Estimates for unprotected left main coronary artery disease vary. One study cited its prevalence in 5% of individuals who underwent coronary angiography. [23] Another study estimated the prevalence at less than 2% of individuals who underwent coronary angiography. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery calcification is a nonspecific term, and the amount of calcification can vary greatly. Most of the population has some degree of calcification and atherosclerosis, though these may not be significant enough to cause significant stenosis. [17] One large study noted calcification in 50% of individuals aged 40 to 49. This number rises to 80% of individuals aged 60 to 69. [18] Gender and age are significant risk factors for coronary calcification. Studies have reported the incidence of coronary calcification at 93% for men older than 70 years and 77% for women aged older than 70. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Left main coronary artery (LMCA) stenosis: These critical lesions have high mortality, given that the LMCA supplies most of the myocardium. In individuals with stable angina, CABG has been the standard of care for these individuals with unprotected LMCA stenosis. The 2013 European Society of Cardiology Guidelines highlighted PCI's role in individuals with a low (< 22) SYNTAX score. [51] The SYNTAX score is widely used and has been validated in many studies. [52] [53] [54] [53] [52] In those experiencing acute coronary syndrome with LMCA culprit lesions, PCI may be an option in those with cardiogenic shock, isolated LMCA culprit lesions, poor surgical candidates, or those with initial TIMI 0 or 1 flow. [55] CABG is preferable in those with concurrent valvular disease, diabetes, decreased left ventricular ejection fraction, extensively calcified LMCA disease, multi-vessel disease, or high SYNTAX score. Recent advances in hemodynamic support with devices such as intra-aortic balloon pumps may make these lesions easier to treat in cases where CABG is inappropriate. These devices can provide hemodynamic support while pursuing PCI.

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary bifurcation lesions are the most common of the complex coronary lesions. Some estimates have reported that these are found in up to 20% of all PCIs. [20] Chronic total occlusions (CTO) are extremely common as well. CTOs are present in 20% of individuals undergoing non-urgent angiography. [21] CTOs are found in 47% of individuals who present with acute coronary syndrome and in 89% of individuals with prior coronary artery bypass grafting. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Multivessel Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Common risk factors for atherosclerosis and CAD include the following: Hypertension Hyperlipidemia Diabetes mellitus Cigarette smoking Age Menopause Hyperhomocysteinemia Family history of coronary artery disease (CAD) Mutations in endothelial nitric oxide synthase [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Restenosis of Stented Coronary Arteries -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for restenosis are classified based on patient features, lesion type, and procedural factors (Table 1). Biological factors associated with an increased risk of restenosis include diabetes mellitus and renal insufficiency. In a multivariate regression analysis, diabetes mellitus in itself increases the risk of BMS-ISR by 30% to 50%. [1] Likewise, the risk of drug-eluting stent ISR (DES-ISR) is increased in diabetic patients when compared to those without diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Saphenous vein graft stenosis: Saphenous venous grafts have accelerated rates of degeneration compared to native coronary arteries, and these stenoses compose about 10% to 15% of PCIs performed. [34] Degeneration of saphenous vein grafts is believed to be due to stress and shearing factors from high arterial flow paired with venous characteristics. Studies have compared the use of bare metal stents, drug-eluting stents, and balloon angioplasty. The SAVED trial found that bare metal stents (BMS) were superior to balloon angioplasty regarding procedural success, reduction in angiographic restenosis, and decreased major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE). [35] The RRISC trial showed that sirolimus-eluting stents reduced the need for target lesion revascularization and late loss. The relative risk reduction of restenosis was about 60%, and the relative risk reduction of need for revascularization was 80%. [11] However, another study by these same authors, the DELAYED RRISC study, revealed a similar need for target lesion revascularization at about 3 years and a significantly lower mortality rate in the BMS group compared to the DES group. [36] Very few studies have shown drug-eluting stents (DES) to be a more effective treatment than BMS in SVG stenosis, with the best evidence for DES in those with stenosis less than 3.5 mm and those with diabetes. [35] Notably, most of these studies were done on early-generation DES, while newer-generation DES have more biocompatible coatings and may have lower rates of stent thrombosis. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Total Occlusion of the Coronary Artery -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for CTO lesion in patients are as below [3] : Known coronary artery disease or history of myocardial infarction Excessive tobacco use High LDL cholesterol, low HDL cholesterol Diabetes Sedentary lifestyle Hypertension Family history of premature disease End-stage kidney disease Obesity Postmenopausal women

retrieved_docs.title: Restenosis of Stented Coronary Arteries -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Biochemical factors such as elevated serum matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), the proteolytic enzymes that degrade the extracellular matrix (ECM), and facilitate the proliferation and migration of endothelial and vascular smooth muscle cells, has been associated with a higher risk of ISR. Hematological indices, including neutrophil/lymphocyte ratio, platelet/lymphocyte ratio, red blood cell distribution width, mean platelet volume, and platelet distribution width, have been hypothesized in some studies to predict the occurrence of increased risk for DES-ISR. [6] The GENetic DEterminants of Restenosis (GENDER) study identified single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in AGTR, GPX1, KAT2B, MMP12, FGB, and VDR genes as being associated with an increased risk of restenosis. [7] Significant mechanical predictors of restenosis include complex lesions ACC/AHA Type B2/C (B2 and C lesions are not only more frequently related to suboptimal acute results but also with a higher restenosis rate and poorer long-term clinical outcomes), [2] the length of the lesion and small vessel diameter. A stent length greater than 35 mm correlates with nearly double the ISR risk compared to less than 20 mm stents. The major risk factors for recurrent ISR are diabetes mellitus, and previous ISR, and type according to Mehran's classification (with an increase from I to IV). [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcified Plaque -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors associated with coronary artery calcification include advanced age, diabetes, kidney disease, and smoking increasing prevalence. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 300,000 individuals undergo coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) in the United States annually. Among those with a saphenous venous graft, 8% to 12% experience early occlusion before discharge. Approximately 15% to 30% of saphenous vein grafts experience occlusion within the first year after CABG. [25] About 60% of individuals develop saphenous vein graft failure within 10 years of surgery. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation should include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Complex Coronary Artery Lesions -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most individuals with complex coronary artery lesions present similarly. The most common presentation is angina pectoris. Essential history elements include evaluating for risk factors of coronary artery disease. It is crucial to ask the patient questions regarding any history of vascular disease, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, age, hyperlipidemia, smoking, and family history of heart disease. Some of the most common symptoms of these lesions include: Chest pain, pressure, tightness, or heaviness (usually substernal or left-sided) radiating to the neck, jaw, left shoulder, or left arm Shortness of breath Palpitations Weakness Light-headedness Nausea or vomiting Sweating Feelings of anxiety or impending doom Indigestion

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 1818
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic nephropathy (اعتلال الكلية السكري) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة), indicating that patients with renal complications of diabetes may be at higher risk for acute coronary ischemic events. Unstable angina, in turn, is clinically associated with valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), suggesting a link between acute myocardial ischemia and underlying valve pathology. Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) is employed as a clinical indicator in the evaluation of valvular heart disease, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing patients with valve abnormalities. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected nature of renal, coronary, and valvular conditions and underscore the importance of comprehensive assessment in patients presenting with any of these disorders.

The coronary arteries (věnčité tepny) also serve as the common site for coronary artery embolism (انصمام الشريان التاجي), coronary artery spasm (تشنج الشريان التاجي), coronary atherosclerotic disease (تصلب عصيدي في التاجي), and coronary artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان التاجي). Recognizing that these acute and chronic cardiovascular conditions—acute coronary insufficiency, acute myocardial injury, supraventricular tachycardia, rheumatic heart disease, chronic heart failure—and systemic diseases such as inflammatory bowel disease and diabetic kidney disease converge on the coronary arterial system and cardiac chambers underscores the importance of integrated assessment of cardiac anatomy, vascular pathology, and systemic comorbidities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم) is clinically associated with unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and with both type 1 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2). This interrelationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and are commonly observed in patients with either form of diabetes. Consequently, careful monitoring and management of lipid levels are essential components of care for individuals with diabetes to mitigate cardiovascular risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: two major sub‑classifications are recognized. One subtype lacks explicit complications and is classified as diabetes mellitus without complication. The other subtype is characterized by diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene and is considered a subset of diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring the broader impact of poorly controlled diabetes on overall health. Organ‑level functional failures commonly co‑occur with secondary diabetes mellitus, including rheumatic mitral valve failure and hepatic‑renal failure, both of which contribute to the patient’s general clinical state. The uncontrolled disease condition reflected in the general clinical state is specifically linked to diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the systemic burden of inadequately managed diabetes. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is closely linked to several cardiac conditions and risk factors. It is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي), hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), and the presence of a heart murmur (نفخة قلبية). Hypercholesterolemia also has a clinical association with cardiac disorder, underscoring its role as a common metabolic risk factor for both entities. Diagnostic evaluation of coronary artery disease frequently employs coronary computed tomography angiography (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب), which itself is related to the broader concept of coronary disease (مرض التاجي). In the hierarchical structure of cardiovascular terminology, the parent concept “أم دم الشريان التاجي” encompasses cardiac disorder as a narrower element and includes coronary disease as a child, indicating that both the specific cardiac disorder and coronary disease are subcategories within this overarching category. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in recognizing the shared pathophysiology, risk profiling, and appropriate imaging strategies for patients with coronary artery disease and related cardiac abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating that patients with coronary atherosclerosis frequently exhibit cardiac abnormalities and that the presence of a cardiac disorder may suggest underlying coronary artery disease, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

These chronic valve lesions contribute to non‑hypertensive congestive heart failure and biologic functional failure, affecting the cardiac chambers (srdeční komory) and heart valves (srdeční chlopně). Therapeutic interventions such as closed heart valvotomy of the mitral valve, other heart valve procedures, and intraventricular pacing target these structural abnormalities. The framework also links chronic rheumatic heart disease to other chronic conditions that share the “chronic” descriptor, including chronic inflammatory small bowel disease and secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic chronic kidney disease, underscoring the relevance of disease chronicity across organ systems. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1495
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with several cardiovascular conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), which is a major risk factor for the development of atherosclerotic plaque in the coronary arteries. Coronary artery disease also co‑occurs with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), reflecting the progression of ischemic damage to cardiac function. Cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) is frequently employed in the assessment and management of coronary artery disease, as well as in the evaluation of related cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), hypercholesterolemia, chest pain (ألم الصَدْر), and neck pain (ألم في العنق). Cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي) themselves show clinical associations with coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia, and the use of cardiac catheterization, underscoring the interconnected nature of these pathologies. Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is commonly observed in patients with hypercholesterolemia and often prompts cardiac catheterization to rule out or treat underlying coronary artery disease. Neck pain (ألم في العنق) may also be a presenting symptom that leads clinicians to perform cardiac catheterization when cardiac involvement is suspected. Overall, hypercholesterolemia, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, various pain presentations, and cardiac catheterization form a tightly linked clinical network, highlighting the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular patient care.

Systemic sequelae—including hepatorenal failure, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus with chronic kidney disease, and the need for extracorporeal circulation with cardiopulmonary bypass—highlight the interplay between rheumatic cardiac disease and multi‑organ dysfunction.
